article_title: NASA Selects Voyager Technologies for the Seventh Private Astronaut Mission, Turning the ISS Into a Dress Rehearsal for Starlab

content_preview.title: NASA Selects Voyager Technologies for the Seventh Private Astronaut Mission, Turning the ISS Into a Dress Rehearsal for Starlab
content_preview.summary: The VOYG-1 flight, targeted to launch no earlier than 2028, gives Voyager a chance to train its ground team and flight controllers before its Starlab commercial station reaches orbit.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

NASA announced on April 15, 2026, that it has selected Voyager Technologies to conduct the seventh private astronaut mission to the International Space Station, according to a [NASA news release](https://www.nasa.gov/news-release/nasa-selects-voyager-for-seventh-private-mission-to-space-station/). The flight, designated VOYG-1, is targeted to launch no earlier than 2028 from Florida and will spend up to 14 days docked at the orbiting laboratory with four crew members. The award make...

editor_notes.content_quality: Clear, well-structured News piece at 825 words (within the 400-1200 range for News). Uses standard section structure: Overview, What We Know, a thematic Starlab section, Context, and What We Don't Know. Claims are individually linked to source anchors throughout.

source_verification.https://www.nasa.gov/news-release/nasa-s: VERIFIED — NASA news release confirms VOYG-1 designation, no-earlier-than-2028 launch from Florida, up to 14 days aboard ISS, four crew members proposed for partner approval, services (crew consumables, cargo delivery, in-orbit storage, sample return with cold transit), the Isaacman quote about 'accelerating the growth of new ideas, industries, and technologies', and the Dylan Taylor attribution framing the award as validating the company's partnership work with NASA. All article claims attributed to NASA are supported.
source_verification.https://aviationweek.com/space/commercia: VERIFIED — confirms announcement at the 41st Space Symposium in Colorado Springs on April 15, 2026; SpaceX as expected transportation provider; the 'practice run at ground operations, training and flight control' framing (attributed in the source to Starlab CEO Marshall Smith, while the article phrases it as 'Voyager officials'—substantively consistent since Starlab Space is Voyager-led, though slightly less precise than the source attribution); Vast as sixth PAM awardee following Axiom's five; and the Isaacman 'send more astronauts to space and ignite the orbital economy' quote.
source_verification.https://www.space.com/space-exploration/: VERIFIED — confirms Starlab cleared PDR in March 2025, 2028 launch target aboard SpaceX Starship, ~12,000 cubic feet of pressurized volume, four-crew capacity with service module, habitat, robotic arm, and experiment racks; $217.5M CLD Phase 1 award, $15M from Texas Space Commission, additional private funding; and the Tim Kopra quote on the design being 'technically sound and safe for astronaut crewed operations'.

editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Every substantive claim is traceable to one of the three verified sources. Dates (April 15, 2026; NET 2028; March 2025 PDR; ISS retirement targeted for 2030), dollar figures ($217.5M, $15M), and specifications (12,000 cu ft, 4 crew, 14 days) all check out against the cited sources. The 'practice run' quote in the article is paraphrased as from 'Voyager officials' where the Aviation Week source attributes it specifically to Starlab CEO Marshall Smith — factually consistent but a sharper attribution would have been cleaner. Not a blocking issue given the Voyager/Starlab organizational relationship.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral, factual reporting. No editorializing or sensationalism. The 'Dress Rehearsal for Starlab' framing in the title is supported directly by the Aviation Week source's 'practice run' quote. The What We Don't Know section appropriately flags gaps (contract value, crew identities, payloads, training scope, ISS retirement window).
editor_notes.originality: Genuinely new story. No prior coverage of the VOYG-1 award, Voyager's PAM selection, or Starlab's 2028 alignment with VOYG-1 in the archive. Previous space coverage focused on unrelated topics (lunar Gateway pause, FAA orbital debris rule, Webb Uranus observations).
editor_notes.sources_allowlist: All three domains (nasa.gov, aviationweek.com, space.com) are present in config/source_allowlist.txt. HTTPS across all sources.
recommendations: Future coverage could attribute the 'practice run' quote specifically to Starlab CEO Marshall Smith rather than the more generic 'Voyager officials' — the primary source names him directly.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: High-quality News submission with cleanly verified sources, accurate claim-to-source mapping, balanced tone, and a strong framing that traces back to named source material. Approved for publication.
